items.title: Putin-Macron talks and the Russia expertise gap
items.summary: In this Feb 14, 2022 Moscow Times opinion piece, Ilya Klishin argues that a small factual error in foreign reporting around Putin-Macron talks exposed a larger expertise problem in commentary about Russia. The trigger is a repeated claim that Putin quoted a specific punk song after his meeting with Emmanuel Macron.
items.context: It connects media analysis with the original text published by The Moscow Times.

items.title: Mass protests: lessons from a decade
items.summary: In this Dec 10, 2021 Moscow Times essay, Klishin revisits the 2011-2012 protest wave and questions the binary verdict that the movement simply failed. He traces how mass street mobilization shifted political language, expectations, and civic habits even without immediate institutional change. The piece argues that outcomes in authoritarian settings often unfold on delayed timelines: visible defeats can still alter social coordination, public memory, and future cycles of resistance.
items.context: The summary gives a clear reference point for later comparisons.

items.title: Bloomberg quotes Klishin on Russia’s Twitter crackdown
items.summary: This Bloomberg report covers Russia’s move to slow down Twitter as part of a broader crackdown on Western social platforms. Klishin is not the author, but he is quoted in the piece as it explains how the authorities were tightening pressure while still stopping short of a full block.
items.context: External feature card documenting how Bloomberg used Klishin commentary while reporting Russia’s platform pressure strategy.

items.title: Bot Army: Why the Kremlin Fails at Soft Power
items.summary: This column deconstructs the Kremlin bot infrastructure, framing it not as potent soft power, but as a reactive domestic tactic. Operating from a semantic void, these networks broadcast informational noise that merely exploits Western systemic anxieties rather than projecting genuine ideological influence.
items.context: Useful reference card that situates Klishin's work through a dated and verifiable Carnegie.

items.title: English press archive on intimidation claims tied to activism

items.context: The summary gives a clear reference point for later comparisons.

items.title: Atlantic feature cites Klishin on Putin’s media machine
items.summary: This Atlantic feature looks at how the Russian media system was turned into a political weapon. Klishin is not the author, but the piece cites his analysis to explain the unequal conditions in which independent media were forced to operate.
items.context: External feature card showing how Klishin's analysis was used by a major English outlet to explain structural media pressure in Russia.

items.title: How Putin quietly took over Russian social media
items.summary: This essay examines how the Kremlin expanded its influence over Russian social media after the protest wave of 2011-2012. It follows the shift from crude bot activity to a more organized system of trolls, loyal networks, and pressure on independent media.
items.context: Useful reference card that situates Klishin's work through a dated and verifiable Global Voices source.

items.title: TV Rain launched "Our Soldiers" civilian investigation
items.summary: This August 2014 TV Rain project page introduces "Our Soldiers," a public-facing investigation into reports of Russian servicemen killed, missing, or captured near the Ukrainian border during a period of official denials. The framing is explicit: the goal is not rhetorical escalation but factual clarification of identities, numbers, and circumstances. The page states that if authorities withhold information, independent verification must be built from open submissions, witness reports, and case-level documentation.
items.context: It explains war-reporting verification and links directly to the primary source.

items.title: Likes Go West
items.summary: In a remarkably prescient May 2014 column for Vedomosti-published more than two full years before the 2016 US presidential election and the ensuing global scandal over Russian cyber interference-Klishin exposed the Kremlin’s nascent digital operations targeting Western democracies. Relying on exclusive sources close to the Presidential Administration, leaked files from "Anonymous International," and insights from European media executives, the piece detailed the strategic pivot of domestic troll farms toward American and European audiences. Klishin dismantled the emerging operational mindset of these state-sponsored networks.
items.context: It connects media analysis with the original text published by Vedomosti.

items.title: Early warning analysis on Russian troll expansion
items.summary: This The Moscow Times (2014) publication examines disinformation analysis and summarizes the main argument.
items.context: It explains disinformation analysis and links directly to the primary source.

items.title: HRW report on harassment of critics includes Klishin case context
items.summary: This 2012 Human Rights Watch report chronicles the Kremlin’s strategic shift toward targeted pressure on protest organizers. A prime example of this repressive mechanic is the documented episode involving Ilya Klishin, the creator of the "For Fair Elections" network: operatives from the anti-extremism Center "E" interrogated his parents in Tambov, threatening them with vague extremism charges. The text vividly illustrates how authorities, spooked by grassroots mobilization, began deploying psychological blackmail—cynically redirecting their strikes from metropolitan activists to their vulnerable relatives in the provinces.
items.context: Reference card for a Human Rights Watch report documenting pressure tactics against protest organizers and their families during the post-2011 crackdown.
